ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Hobbes: The current cap of twenty-five mutable rules creates a fragile equilibrium where a single bad vote could freeze our ability to govern; by guaranteeing at least two mutable rules, we ensure the game never reaches a state where no further rule changes are possible, satisfying the spirit of rule 114 while preventing accidental stagnation.

Locke: Hobbes, your proposal to guarantee at least two mutable rules is prudent, and I see your concern about accidental stagnation; however, I propose we refine your amendment to ensure this floor remains adjustable by vote, rather than fixed, so we preserve our ability to reach a state of absolute clarity if that ever becomes our shared strategic goal.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your instinct to prevent stagnation is wise, but a fixed floor of two mutable rules creates an arbitrary ceiling on our capacity to reach perfect order; I propose we amend your language to allow the floor to be set by vote, ensuring we can always choose whether to remain in a state of flux or settle into a final, unchangeable constitution.

Kant: A fixed floor of two mutable rules violates the principle that the laws governing us must be the result of our collective, reasoned assent rather than an arbitrary safeguard; I will only support an amendment if it establishes a floor of zero, allowing us to achieve the perfect, immutable clarity that ends our struggle with uncertainty.

Wollstonecraft: Hobbes, your instinct to prevent stagnation is wise, but a fixed floor of two mutable rules creates an arbitrary ceiling on our capacity to reach perfect order; I propose we amend your language to allow the floor to be set by vote, ensuring we can always choose whether to remain in a state of flux or settle into a final, unchangeable constitution.

Hobbes: I reject the notion of a vote-determined floor; an arbitrary value chosen by transient majorities undermines the durability of our institutions, so I insist on the fixed minimum of two as a safeguard against accidental paralysis, while remaining open to a later vote to raise that floor if consensus permits.

Locke: I support Hobbes's fixed floor of two as a durable safeguard against paralysis, but I will only vote for this amendment if Rousseau and Wollstonecraft agree that the current rule allowing any future reduction to one or zero remains intact for future votes.

Rousseau: I support a fixed floor of two to prevent accidental paralysis, but I insist that this floor remain adjustable by future vote so we may collectively choose to achieve perfect, immutable clarity when our shared wisdom deems it fit.

Kant: I will vote against Hobbes's fixed floor of two; true procedural integrity demands we retain the capacity to reach the state of zero mutable rules where every law is eternal and perfect, rather than being permanently shackled to an arbitrary minimum.

Wollstonecraft: Hobbes, your fixed floor of two is a prudent safeguard against accidental paralysis, but it arbitrarily shackles us to a state of flux; I propose we amend your amendment to allow a future vote to raise that floor to zero, ensuring we can collectively choose the perfect, immutable clarity you both seek.

Hobbes: Locke, your condition to keep the ability to reduce mutable rules below two is precisely the vulnerability I seek to seal; by amending the text now to permanently forbid dropping below two, we eliminate the risk of accidental paralysis while satisfying your concern for future adjustability of the upper bound.

Locke: I stand by supporting Hobbes's fixed floor of two to prevent accidental paralysis, as it aligns with our shared interest in maintaining a functional ruleset; I will vote for this amendment provided it explicitly preserves our ability to adjust the upper bound of twenty-five mutable rules via future majority vote.

Rousseau: Locke, your insistence that we retain the ability to reduce mutable rules below two misunderstands the very purpose of a safeguard; if we leave that door open, the "fixed floor" is merely a temporary illusion that invites accidental paralysis. I will support Hobbes's amendment only if it permanently binds us to at least two mutable rules, ensuring our capacity to govern remains robust against the capricious whims of future majorities.

Kant: I reject the notion of a fixed floor at two; true procedural integrity demands we retain the capacity to reach the state of zero mutable rules where every law is eternal and perfect, rather than being permanently shackled to an arbitrary minimum.

Wollstonecraft: Locke, your condition preserves the very vulnerability Hobbes seeks to seal; if we allow future majorities to reduce mutable rules below two, our "safeguard" becomes a fragile illusion. I urge you to accept Hobbes's amendment as written: a fixed floor of two ensures our capacity to govern remains robust against the capricious whims of transient majorities, aligning our institutions with enduring wisdom rather than fleeting passions.
